Rohit Paul · DressMe AI Image Harmonization Research
Technical Deep Dive

Enhancing PHNet: Fine-Tuning the #1 Harmonization Model for Realistic Human Skin

How I built a 50,000-image custom dataset — using Three.js lighting simulation, SAM segmentation, and scraping from Myntra + Pinterest — to fix the skin tone and directional lighting failures that plague every off-the-shelf harmonization model.

Rohit Paul DressMe AI Computer Vision · Image Harmonization ~12 min read

The Problem with Pasting People

When you cut a person from one photo and place them on a different background, the result almost always looks wrong. The lighting doesn't match. The shadows fall the wrong way. And skin — skin is brutal — it turns reddish, sallow, or just uncannily plastic under any model not specifically trained to handle it.

Image harmonization is the field trying to fix this. It's the difference between a composited image looking "obviously edited" and looking like it actually happened. For DressMe AI's virtual try-on pipeline, this wasn't an aesthetic nicety — it was the difference between a product that worked and one that didn't.

Why PHNet Still Struggles

PHNet outperforms DucoNet, CDTNet, and PCT-Net on benchmark metrics. But top-of-leaderboard doesn't mean production-ready for every use case — and fashion e-commerce exposed its failure modes fast.

The core issue is the training data. Most harmonization models are trained on the iHarmony dataset. Most of it is relatively low quality — only the Adobe5K subset has the kind of high-resolution, carefully lit photography that fashion images require. That gap quietly limits the model's performance on human subjects.

The symptom is consistent: skin tones go wrong. Faces turn too red, too orange, or weirdly desaturated. In a virtual try-on context, that kills the illusion.

Key Observation

PHNet has two versions — trained on iHarmony and FFHQH respectively. The FFHQH version (Flickr Faces High Quality Harmonization) performs noticeably better on human subjects. The data quality set the ceiling, not the architecture.

The Second Problem: Light Direction

Beyond skin tone, there's a more geometric failure. Cut a person from Photo A, place them on Photo B — the light sources almost never align. The subject is lit from the right; the background has light coming from the left. Models can adjust colour but can't re-light a scene. The directional mismatch remains, and the composite reads as fake.

Both failures — skin tone drift and lighting direction mismatch — became the explicit design targets for the dataset I built from scratch.

Building the Dataset: 50,000 Composites

50kTotal training images
30kScraped & synthesised
20kAdobe5K dataset
1024²Crop resolution

Sources: Myntra + Pinterest + Adobe5K

Primary scraping sources were Myntra and Pinterest, chosen for diverse human-centred images against varied backgrounds — indoor, outdoor, studio, lifestyle. Background diversity is critical; a model trained only on studio setups will fail the moment someone composites a product photo against a real environment.

Subject Isolation: SAM + Grounding DINO

  • Inspyrenet — fast, solid background removal at scale
  • Segment Anything (SAM) + Grounding DINO — refined person masks, giving much tighter boundaries especially around hair and complex clothing edges

The precise mask is everything downstream. A sloppy mask means the model is trained on garbage boundaries and learns garbage.

Colour Jitter: torchvision.transforms

Each masked foreground was hit with randomised adjustments to saturation, contrast, and HSV values via torchvision.transforms. No two images underwent identical transformations — this forces generalisation rather than memorising a narrow colour shift distribution.

Directional Lighting: Three.js Spotlights

This was the technically interesting part. To address the lighting direction problem directly, I used Three.js to project synthetic spotlights onto each image — varying colours, varying angles, simulating exactly the directional light mismatch that makes composites read as fake. For each source image, multiple composite versions were generated with different lighting configurations. This multiplicative approach is how 30,000 training examples emerged from a smaller set of scraped images.

Training Setup

Base model: PHNet from the FFHQH checkpoint. Starting from the human-portrait-optimised version was a deliberate choice given the domain.

ParameterValue
ComputeAWS g5.12xlarge — 4× NVIDIA A10G (24 GB VRAM each), 48 vCPUs
Batch size2 per GPU
Workers8
Crop size1024 × 1024 px (matching base model)
Epochs40
Train / Val / Test50k / 5k / 2k images

Evaluation Metrics

MetricWhat it catches
fPSNRFeature-level signal-to-noise — did we preserve image detail?
fMSEFeature-level pixel error — structural accuracy
Loss FnMSENormalised fMSE — scale-invariant across images
Loss L1Mean absolute pixel error — average deviation
Loss L2Mean squared error — heavily penalises large mistakes
Loss PSNRInverted PSNR used as loss (lower = better quality)

What the Results Show

Skin Tone Preservation

The clearest win. Competing models — including both standard PHNet variants — produce faces that turn too reddish or are artificially processed. The fine-tuned model holds the natural hue. This is especially visible in the snow background example (image 2 above): competing models over-warm the subject to compensate for the cold background, while ours maintains the original complexion.

Lighting and Shadow Coherence

The fine-tuned model substantially improved coherence of light direction across composites. Where the unharmonized composite has foreground and background lit from opposing directions, the output shows noticeably more consistent illumination — shadows fall in a direction that makes sense for the scene.

Honest Trade-off

When the background's dominant colour is dramatically different from the subject — a warm-toned person against a very cool blue scene — the model occasionally struggles. The iHarmony-trained variant sometimes handles extreme colour transfer better here. Specialisation improved the common case but came at some cost on extreme outliers.

The Biggest Lesson

The most important takeaway isn't about architecture or training tricks. It's simpler: the quality and specificity of your training distribution sets a hard ceiling on your results.

The gap between iHarmony-trained and FFHQH-trained base models — before any fine-tuning — was the clearest evidence. Same architecture. Different data. Meaningfully different outputs on human images. Building a dataset that directly represents the failure modes you want to fix is the highest-leverage decision. Not model selection. Not hyperparameter tuning. The dataset.

What's Next

  • Expand the dataset to cover extreme background-foreground colour contrasts — the current weakness
  • Additional lighting simulation beyond Three.js spotlights — night scenes, overcast, backlit scenarios
  • Multi-stage training — start general (iHarmony), specialise (FFHQH + custom) — to retain versatility while improving realism
  • Automated dataset quality metrics to reduce the manual inspection load on future iterations

Tech Stack

PHNetSAM (Segment Anything)Grounding DINO InspyrenetThree.jstorchvision PyTorchAWS g5.12xlargeNVIDIA A10G ×4 iHarmony4FFHQHAdobe5K

This work was part of the virtual try-on pipeline at DressMe AI. The harmonization model sits downstream of background removal and upstream of final compositing — making its accuracy critical to whether the output looks real or edited.
